data.title: Hong Kong medical student gets 1 year probation for filming men in dorm showers
data.description:
A medical student from Hong Kong’s top university has been sentenced to a year’s probation after admitting to secretly filming two hallmates while they were showering in a dormitory bathroom.
Chung Chun-yu, 23, earlier pleaded guilty to two counts of voyeurism and told Principal Magistrate David Cheung Chi-wai that his act of using a mobile phone to film other students through a hole in a laundry basket at a University of Hong Kong hall in September last year was a one-off incident.
The defendant said he did not suffer from voyeuristic disorder but admitted to police that he was attracted to the male body and had made the recordings to satisfy his sexual desires.
Eastern Court heard on Friday that a probation officer’s report found Chung had shown genuine remorse. Magistrate Cheung also noted that the student had taken steps to address his issues by undergoing therapy.
“Value this second chance,” Cheung told the defendant as he imposed the probation sentence.
On the night of September 22, 2024, a 23-year-old male resident of the Madam S H Ho Residence for Medical Students discovered a mobile phone covered by a towel in a laundry basket that had been placed covertly nearby.
He later found Chung hiding in a cubicle and confronted him at the scene.
The device had recorded a 39-minute video showing the naked bodies of the resident and another male in the shower. The resident subsequently reported the matter to police.

data.title: Evergrande liquidators set November-end deadline for bids on property services unit
data.description:
Liquidators for the delisted China Evergrande Group have asked “selected bidders” to submit their offers by the end of the month for a property management unit they have been seeking to sell, according to a filing to the Hong Kong stock exchange on Friday.
The deadline for the offer, as well as identifying bidders for China Evergrande’s stake in Evergrande Property Services (EPS), comes as creditors seek to recover assets from the Guangzhou-based developer, which collapsed under the weight of more than US$300 billion in liabilities.
The liquidators cautioned that any potential transaction “remains at a preliminary stage, and the timing for entering the next phase of the process and submitting the final proposal have yet to be determined by the liquidators”.
Shares of EPS fell 2.3 per cent to HK$1.25 at the lunch break on Friday.
EPS, which has a market capitalisation of HK$13.5 billion (US$1.7 billion), reported a profit of 473.2 million yuan (US$66.6 million) in the six months to June, a 5.6 per cent decline from a year earlier, according to its latest financial results.
In its annual report, EPS said it had signed new projects spanning more than 23 million square metres with annual contract revenue worth about 600 million yuan. Non-residential projects accounted for more than 70 per cent of revenue. It had some 3,000 projects under operation last year, according to the annual report.
China Evergrande’s liquidators control a 51 per cent holding in EPS.
China Evergrande’s Hong Kong shares were ejected from the stock exchange on August 25 following the bourse’s decision to delist the group.
Evergrande’s shares were suspended from trading on January 29, 2024 – the same day a Hong Kong court ordered its liquidation after it failed to present a viable restructuring plan.
Under the exchange’s rules, a company that remains suspended for 18 consecutive months is subject to delisting.
That marked the end of Evergrande’s 16-year trading status in Hong Kong, which spanned an era when the company was the poster child of mainland China’s property boom.
The Guangzhou-based developer fell from its perch in 2020 after Beijing rolled out the three red lines policy, which reined in overleveraged developers to cool down the mainland’s housing market.

data.title: Debates welcomed at Legco election forums, but no ‘mudslinging, back-stabbing’
data.description:
Hong Kong’s No 2 official has said that debates are welcomed at government-run Legislative Council election forums while defending campaign initiatives as efforts to elect high-quality candidates without them smearing each other.
Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki also said on Friday that he hoped all employees could cast their vote on December 7 even if they had work obligations and that no one would be asked to disclose whether they voted or not.
Chan’s defence followed a series of “peaceful” forums held by the administration this week and a recent call by the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office – Beijing’s top office overseeing the city – urging candidates to “never turn to spreading rumours, leaking information, smear campaigns, attacks and slander, or tearing apart communities”.
“There is no problem with candidates expressing different views during debates. And it is their decision to choose the way they present themselves – whether in an argumentative approach or not,” Chan told a radio programme on Friday.
“But we hope candidates will not resort to tactics like smearing, mudslinging and back-stabbing.”
The government revealed plans on Monday to host 39 election forums – one each for the 10 geographical, 28 functional and one Election Committee constituencies – ahead of polling day for the city’s second “patriot-only” legislature, when 90 new seats would be elected.
This is the first time the government has taken the lead in organising election forums, which were previously mainly run by local media outlets.
As of Thursday, six forums for six geographical constituencies were held. But most candidates refrained from challenging their opponents, limiting the questions to asking for suggestions on different issues – a stark difference to forums held by broadcasters in the past where crossfire among candidates was common.
A total of 51 candidates are competing for the 20 seats in ten geographical constituencies where residents will cast their ballots.
Another 60 are vying for 30 seats in the functional constituencies, and the remaining 50 for 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency. These seats are decided by sector-specific voters.
The No 2 official highlighted that there was no difference between forums organised by the government and the media outlets as they shared similar procedures and rules.

Media outlets, including major broadcasters, have cancelled their election forums earlier as candidates pulled out one after another since the government announced its forum plan.
“We take the initiative to organise forums this time because there are many candidates and they need to be given opportunities to present their manifestos as time is running out,” Chan said, referring to the election day that is three weeks away.
Chan also responded to the government’s recent all-out efforts to encourage voters to cast their ballots, saying the work was done because the Legco played a key role in the city’s governance.
“But the Legco will be elected despite the high or low turnout rate. The most important thing is that [we need] a legislature of high quality,” he said.
The 2021 Legco election – the first held under Beijing’s “patriots-only” overhaul – drew a record low turnout of 30.2 per cent.
The city’s private sector has also joined the government in drumming up momentum for the election, with several major property developers such as Henderson Land and Sino Group, granting half-day paid leave and other conveniences to facilitate employees to vote.
“I don’t want to see that an employee is unable to vote because he needs to go to work,” Chan said. “The more facilitation, the better.
“But certainly there would not be situations where voters are asked to disclose if they vote or whom they vote for.”
In a commentary published on Monday, the HKMAO said all approved candidates were “outstanding talent who love the country and Hong Kong, possess a sense of responsibility, demonstrate capability, and have ambition”.
But the office urged all candidates to campaign vigorously and jointly build a positive election culture through improving their political platforms, conducting in-depth exchanges with voters and building more social consensus.
“[Candidates] should firmly reject bad practices such as factionalism and cliquism. [They] must not follow the anti-China and Hong Kong forces who in the past spread rumours, leaked malicious information, launched personal attacks, and divided the community,” it said.

data.title: ‘Violent Antifa’: US designates 4 left-wing groups in Europe as terrorists
data.description:
The United States on Thursday designated four entities in Germany, Italy and Greece as global terrorists, accusing them of being “violent Antifa groups” as US President Donald Trump takes aim at left-wing groups.
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said in a statement he had designated German-based Antifa Ost as “Specially Designated Global Terrorists”, along with three other groups in Greece and Italy, in an effort to build on Trump’s “commitment to confront Antifa’s campaign of political violence”.
Rubio said he intends to also designate the groups as “Foreign Terrorist Organisations” from November 20 and warned that Washington would target other groups around the world.
“Groups affiliated with this movement ascribe to revolutionary anarchist or Marxist ideologies, including anti-Americanism, ‘anti-capitalism’, and anti-Christianity, using these to incite and justify violent assaults domestically and overseas,” Rubio said.
“The United States will continue using all available tools to protect our national security and public safety and will deny funding and resources to terrorists, including targeting other Antifa groups across the globe.”
Trump and his Republican allies have accused antifa followers of fomenting political violence following the September assassination of conservative activist Charlie Kirk and amid protests against federal immigration authorities in cities including Los Angeles, Chicago and Portland, Oregon.

Trump threatened to pursue antifa during his 2017-2021 term in office, and in September called antifa a terrorist organisation in an executive order.
Antifa, short for “anti-fascist”, is a decentralised movement without a clear structure, command hierarchy or leader, according to a 2020 Congressional Research Service report.
The State Department said Antifa Ost conducted numerous attacks against those it “perceives as ‘fascists’ or part of the ‘right-wing scene’ in Germany between 2018 and 2023 and is accused of having conducted a series of attacks in Budapest in mid-February 2023”.
The German foreign and interior ministries were not immediately available for comment.
Germany’s domestic intelligence service identified the Antifa Ost group as a violent network in a 2024 report.
Four people connected to the group were arrested between December 2023 and November 2024, accused of targeting those they considered right-wing extremists in violent attacks.
While the arrests are thought to have weakened the group, the 2024 report warned that its members continued to target Germany’s far-right AfD party, which has courted ties with the Trump administration.
In addition to Antifa Ost in Germany, Washington designated as terrorists the Informal Anarchist Federation/International Revolutionary Front in Italy as well as Armed Proletarian Justice and Revolutionary Class Self-Defence in Greece, according to a separate statement.
The Italian prime minister’s office said it had no immediate comment. A Greek official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the government works to minimise terrorist acts. “We are against any terrorist act and terrorist organisation,” the official said.
The Revolutionary Class Self-Defence group said it carried out an April explosion at railway operator Hellenic Train. The explosion caused minor damage and no injuries.
The group also claimed responsibility for a 2024 attack on the labour ministry in Athens, which also caused no injuries after police evacuated the area following a warning.
The State Department said Armed Proletarian Justice claimed responsibility for planting a bomb near the Greek riot police headquarters in Goudi in 2023.

data.title: Haughey fires 100m freestyle warning shot, coach Rushton ‘really excited’ for what comes next
data.description:
Siobhan Haughey fired a warning shot at her rivals on Friday, posting the fastest time in the heats for the women’s 100m freestyle, little more than 12 hours after storming to National Games gold.
And the man who has been at Haughey’s side pushing her to improve said there was more to come from the 28-year-old, despite describing her performance in the 200m freestyle on Thursday as “one of her best swims” ever.
Haughey displayed her supremacy in her first China Games, leading almost from the start to touch the wall in one minute, 54.85 seconds, nearly a second ahead of her closest rival, Li Bingjie.
With an identical gold medal hanging around his neck, coach Tom Rushton was visibly delighted before he started praising the city’s history maker.
“It was great to see her execute really well and have a swim that’s one of the best swims that she’s ever had,” he said.

The experienced coach, however, admitted there had been “some unknowns” for both of them coming into the event.
“She’d been injured in the summer and we hadn’t had the typical run-up with some high-level competitions before this, so we were a little bit anxious even though I saw that she was in good shape in the training,” the English-born Canadian said. “But until you get that first race, you never really know.
“Usually we would have a little bit more training behind us, especially for the 200m before we come to a major meet. But because of her injury, we didn’t have that much time.”
After reeling in two Olympic bronze medals in Paris last summer, and adding another short-course World Championships title to her name in Budapest last December, Haughey took a few months off before resuming training in April.
An injury then ruled her out of the World Aquatics Championships in Singapore in July, sparking concerns over whether her glittering career was coming to an end.
But Rushton hinted there could be more to come from the pair, who have been working together since after the Tokyo Games in 2021.
“I think her training has been very good and we’re trying to build a little bit slowly so that we don’t get injured again,” he said. “But we have lots of space as we run up to the [2026] Asian Games, World Championships and perhaps the [2028 LA] Olympics.
“So we’ve planned to be a little bit cautious and a little bit gentle. We hope that we continue to train and we continue to get better and we can continue to get faster. We try to swim as fast as possible all the time.”
First though comes the push for 100m gold, and the Hongkonger cruised into Friday’s semi-finals, finishing in 54.29 seconds at the Shenzhen Universiade Sports Centre.
Her biggest rival in the evening session is likely to be Yang Junxuan, who touched the wall in 54.58, but set China’s national record of 52.48 during the first leg of the women’s 4x100m freestyle relay at the Paris Olympics.
Haughey, though, has a personal best of 52.02, which is also the Asian record, a mark she set in 2023.
“I’m really interested to see how the next few races go,” Rushton said. “We’ve got the 100m starting tomorrow morning. I think that could be really exciting and really fun.”

data.title: China’s rare earths breakthrough, Fujian carrier’s naval genius: SCMP’s 7 highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from this week’s news across Hong Kong, mainland China, the wider Asia region and beyond that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. In world first, Chinese scientists find rare earth minerals from a living plant
A Chinese-led team of scientists says it has found a naturally formed mineral with rare earth elements in a fern, a world first that offers a “green circular model” for extracting high-value rare earths, according to an institution behind the study. The researchers said the discovery of nanoscale monazite in a living plant “opens new possibilities for the direct recovery of functional rare earth element materials”.
2. Beijing vows to strike back if Japan uses military force in Taiwan Strait
Beijing has warned that any Japanese military involvement in the Taiwan Strait would be regarded as “an act of aggression” and vowed to strike back in response, marking the latest twist in a diplomatic spat over Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan.
3. Meet Ma Weiming, the Chinese naval genius behind the Fujian’s catapult

Only a select few People’s Liberation Army officers attended last week’s commissioning of the Fujian, but among them was Rear Admiral Ma Weiming – the man whose technological breakthrough put the aircraft carrier among the most advanced of its kind.
4. Journal defends work with fake AI citations amid Hong Kong university probe
An academic journal that published a paper containing fictitious AI-generated references has said the work’s core conclusions remained valid despite “some mismatches and inaccuracies” with the citations, following an investigation launched by the University of Hong Kong, where the author is studying for her PhD.
5. Malaysian police defend bodyguards’ viral leap onto moving car at Asean summit
Two female bodyguards have set Malaysian social media abuzz after a video showing them leaping onto a moving SUV during the recent Asean summit went viral – a moment the police have since defended as standard and professionally executed security protocol.
6. McDonald’s Hong Kong shops sell like hotcakes, yielding US$23.5 million

Two McDonald’s shops in Hong Kong have been sold to the same buyer for HK$183 million (US$23.5 million), marking the fast-food chain’s second and third sales this month as it proceeds with an asset-disposal plan amid an improving commercial property market.
7. China guest floods room after cancellation denied, pays 280 times US$15 room rate
A Chinese woman who flooded her hotel room on purpose after her request to cancel her reservation was denied ended up paying 280 times more than the original room rate. A hotel in China’s southern island province Hainan called the police on October 28, reporting that a woman had flooded one of their rooms.

data.title: Peking duck is a centuries-old favourite – how do Hong Kong’s top chefs make theirs?
data.description:
Marinated in a rich blend of Sichuan spices and roasted until the skin transforms into a crackling golden armour, the flaming Peking duck at Hutong restaurant in Hong Kong’s Tsim Sha Tsui neighbourhood is a feast for the senses.
Paired with standard companions such as pancakes, hoisin sauce and cucumber, the duck also comes with a side dish of drama: it is flambéed and carved at the table with a pianya dao – a traditional knife with a long, thin, rectangular blade designed to slice duck meat into precise portions.
While precise air-drying and roasting are key steps to achieving the crispy skin, finishing the cooking with fire gives it extra snap, says Hutong’s barbecue head chef, Ho Wing-cheong. The crunch factor is crucial.
Thin crispy skin is a vital element of Peking duck, with some arguing it is more important than the meat. As one of China’s most important national dishes, it comes wrapped in tradition.
Peking duck is said to have originated in Hangzhou in the 13th century, gaining imperial favour during the Ming dynasty (1368-1644) after the capital moved to Peking (modern-day Beijing) in the 15th century.
Techniques were refined, and by the 18th century, it was a national dish, served in high-end restaurants, with establishments such as Bianyifang (founded in 1416) and Quanjude (founded in 1864) developing distinct roasting methods.

“Peking duck has inspired poetry, been a staple for the ruling class for generations, and even has its own museum in Beijing,” Ho says, referring to a museum in the Quanjude Roast Duck Restaurant, a chain famous for its Peking duck.
The dish has also been embroiled in what has been coined “duck diplomacy” following the former US president Richard Nixon’s 1972 visit to China to smooth strained relations.
The Chinese government welcomed Nixon with a banquet that included Peking duck. Nixon loved it, and the dish was credited with helping to improve relations in one of the earliest examples of “gastrodiplomacy”.
Ho says the traditional method of preparing Peking duck, called menlu, involved a closed brick oven that was heated by burning fruitwood. The fire burned out, and the embers’ heat roasted the birds.
In the 1860s, a new method, gualu – when ducks were hung inside an open oven – became popular. The debate on which method is best continues to this day, he says.
Today, the recipe calls for a whole duck, with the head attached, he says: “Air is pumped under the skin, the duck is slathered in a sugary syrup and various spices and left to dry overnight.” It is then roasted.
At Hutong, apples are used to boost the flavour, and the fructose in the juice tenderises the meat. It takes just 20 minutes to marinate, then the apples are removed before the duck is roasted.
Our pick of places serving Peking duck in Hong Kong
Mott 32

Indulgent sums up Mott 32’s “Celebration Duck”, which showcases eight premium cuts from its birds, which are imported from Beijing. Each piece has been selected for the just-right ratio of crispy skin, succulent meat and rendered fat.
Served on custom-made buns and crowned with a dollop of premium caviar, the dish delivers a rich, salty-sweet bite, says Mott 32 co-founder Malcolm Wood.
“Celebration Duck is a bold reimagining of how Peking duck can be savoured – luxurious, modern and meant to be shared,” Wood says, adding that the dish took months of trial and error to perfect the crispy skin and moist meat.
“We tried 30 types of duck to find the best one, dozens of woods to smoke it, and a myriad of different recipes and cooking techniques to achieve the best one.” They settled on apple wood for its sweet and smoky aroma.
Every Mott 32 – from Vancouver to Singapore and Dubai to Bangkok – has a custom-built duck oven and storage fridge to ensure the perfect preparation and cooking.
“We wanted to stay true to tradition and ensure our Peking duck is exactly like you would expect in the best restaurants in Beijing, but with a modern twist of apple wood smoke to add a rich, umami flavour to the skin.”
Standard Chartered Bank Building, 4-4A Des Voeux Road Central, Central. Tel: 2885 8688
Lung King Heen

Executive Chinese chef Chan Yan-tak has spent two decades perfecting Peking duck. It is all about finding the ideal balance of meat and fat, he says.
“Wrappers for our Peking duck are home-made, complemented by a flavourful duck sauce and duck meat that enhances the dish,” says Chan, adding that the hoisin sauce stands out with its distinct blend of ginger, spring onions, garlic and sesame paste.
Lung King Heen, too, serves up tradition.
“The tableside preparation and serving of Peking duck is a time-honoured culinary tradition, often regarded as a symbol of Chinese cuisine. It showcases the skill and artistry involved in its theatrical preparation,” Chan says.
4/F, Four Seasons Hotel Hong Kong, 8 Finance Street, Central. Tel: 3196 8882
Lai Ching Heen

Peking duck is one of the restaurant’s most well-known dishes, says executive chef Lau Yiu-fai, who was inspired to reimagine the traditional presentation of sliced skin with pancakes after a work trip to Japan many years ago.
“I introduced a more elegant approach: crispy duck skin ornately fanned out, delicate flour wrappers served in a steamer, and refreshing accompaniments such as pineapple and green papaya alongside traditional cucumber julienne and an array of sauces.”
This, he says, creates new layers of flavour while honouring the essence of the dish. “From the moment it debuted, our version of Peking duck became an instant favourite, and has been a permanent feature on the à la carte menu ever since.”
Lau says while the audience for Chinese cuisine today is different from the past, with guests appreciating aesthetics and presentation, something that has not changed is the importance of temperature. Dishes, he says, must be served piping hot to truly respect the cuisine.
“Our Peking duck is one of Lai Ching Heen’s most popular dishes, and its enduring popularity shows just how important it is to preserve tradition while continuing to refine it for today’s diners.”
Regent Hong Kong, 18 Salisbury Road, Tsim Sha Tsui. Tel: 2313 2313
Duddell’s

For a duck’s skin to become crisp, it has to go through a meticulous preparation process.
At Duddell’s in Central, Beijing-sourced birds first have their wings and feet trimmed, followed by a thorough cleaning of the oesophagus, lungs and kidneys. The birds are soaked in lightly salted water for an hour to remove gamy odours and blood while preserving the original flavour.
Each duck is then blanched and strapped to a hook to dry until its body temperature is consistent with the ambient temperature. “It is roasted at 230 degrees Celsius [446 degrees Fahrenheit] for around 30 minutes, depending on the size of the duck, the day’s weather – temperature and humidity – and the chef’s judgment,” says a Duddell’s spokesman.
Servings come in various styles: one with all the traditional condiments plus pancakes, or option two, with either minced duck, lettuce wraps or sautéed Peking duck pieces in Duddell’s signature XO sauce.
3/F and 4/F, 1 Duddell Street, Central. Tel: 2525 9191
Xin Rong Ji

Specialising in Taizhou cuisine, this Michelin-star restaurant has also carved a reputation for Peking duck. Remember to pre-order and show up exactly at the arranged time.
138 Lockhart Road, Wan Chai. Tel: 3462 3516
Man Wah

Man Wah’s Peking duck is carved at the table and served with classic pancakes, cucumber, spring onion and seafood sauce.
25/F, Mandarin Oriental, Hong Kong, 5 Connaught Road Central, Central. Tel: 2825 4003
Sha Tin 18

The Peking duck is sliced in front of guests and divided into three dishes: crispy duck skin, duck breast meat and duck leg meat.
4/F, Hyatt Regency Hong Kong, Sha Tin, 18 Chak Cheung Street, Sha Tin. Tel: 3723 7932
Ho Lee Fook
Called Ho Lee Duck, the dish here undergoes a preparation closer to Cantonese roast meat than typical Peking duck. It is served with pancakes, hoisin sauce, cucumber, house pickles and mala beef sauce.
3-5 Elgin Street, Central. Tel: 2810 0860

data.title: China’s housing slide deepens as October prices fall at fastest pace in a year
data.description:
The decline in China’s home prices is accelerating, with new home prices in October – traditionally a peak month for sales – falling at the fastest pace in 12 months across the country despite government support, as the economy slows and job prospects remain uncertain.
New home prices fell 0.5 per cent month on month on average across 70 sampled cities, according to National Bureau of Statistics data released on Friday. It was the steepest drop since the 0.7 per cent fall in September last year. Year on year, prices slipped 2.6 per cent in October, compared with a 2.7 per cent drop in September.
Only six out of the 70 cities posted gains in October over September.
Primary home prices in top-tier cities, which are more economically developed and typically have higher housing demand, fell 0.3 per cent. Shanghai was the sole exception among the four top-tier cities, posting an increase of 0.3 per cent, while prices in Beijing, Shenzhen, and Guangzhou declined by 0.1 per cent, 0.7 per cent, and 0.8 per cent, respectively.
New home prices in second-tier cities fell 0.4 per cent in October, while those in third-tier cities eased 0.5 per cent.

The country’s real estate sector has been on a downward spiral since late 2020 when Beijing implemented the “three red lines” campaign that tightened financing for developers in an effort to cool a red-hot market. In that year, the top 100 developers sold a total of 11.5 trillion yuan (US$1.62 trillion) worth of homes, according to China Index Academy. By 2024, that figure had fallen by more than 60 per cent to 4.4 trillion yuan.
The downturn has affected everything from employment to consumption. Many Chinese developers, especially the privately owned ones, have been squeezed by liquidity pressures and debt defaults.
Falling primary home prices reflected the fact that developers, under mounting financial pressure, were slashing prices to recover cash, analysts said.
“Many developers, including state-owned ones, now prioritise cash flow over profits, preferring to secure funds rather than wait for better prices,” said Raymond Cheng, an independent property analyst.
Authorities have rolled out a series of measures to resuscitate the real estate sector, which at its peak contributed up to a quarter of the country’s gross domestic product. Local governments nationwide had issued more than 510 supportive policies so far this year, according to data compiled by China Index Academy.
Between August and September, Beijing, Shanghai and Shenzhen eased home purchase restrictions, which were originally imposed during China’s property boom to curb spiralling prices. In June, Guangzhou became the first top city to fully scrap all home purchase limits.
In the next five-year plan announced last month, Beijing pledged to boost domestic consumption and called for the removal of unreasonable restrictions on the purchase of homes and cars.
In the secondary home market, prices have been falling for more than two years. Prices dropped 0.7 per cent in October from September and 5.4 per cent year on year. First-tier cities saw the steepest monthly drop at 1.0 per cent.
There were more transactions in big cities compared with smaller cities, but at discounted prices, according to Cheng. Some households which owned multiple properties had been diversifying, including into stock markets, he added.
“Put simply, the process of deflating the housing bubble is still ongoing in cities across the country,” according to Yan Yuejin, vice-president of E-House China Real Estate Research Institute in Shanghai. But it could also mean an opportunity to buy properties at cheaper cost, coupled with “historically low down payment requirements”.

data.title: China lets fly world’s largest kite in Inner Mongolia for electric power generation
data.description:
China has successfully deployed the world’s largest power-generating kite, capable of harnessing the potential of energy-dense, high-altitude winds to provide cheaper power than traditional surface turbines.
The 5,000 square-metre (53,820 square-foot) high-altitude wind power capture kite was unfurled at a test site at Alxa Left Banner in the Inner Mongolia autonomous region on Wednesday, according to state broadcaster CCTV.
Resembling a parachute, the power-generating kite was developed by the China Energy Engineering Corporation as part of the country’s first national research and development project for high-altitude wind power.
Tethered to a generator on the ground, the kite can fly at an altitude exceeding 300 metres (984 feet).
During the tests, the 5,000 square-metre kite – about the size of 11 standard NBA basketball courts – as well as two connected 1,200 square-metre kites were lifted into the air by helium balloons and successfully deployed and retracted.

Cao Lun, chief commander of the national high-altitude wind project, told state news agency Xinhua on Wednesday that the experiment measured the tension of the kite “in natural wind conditions when it is open and then calculated its opening characteristics”.
Cao said the tests of both types of kites would provide important data for the design and optimisation of a ladder-like wind energy system.
The wind power kites could be deployed at heights exceeding 5,000 metres and cut land use by 95 per cent as well as reduce steel consumption by 90 per cent compared with traditional onshore wind projects, according to CCTV.
They could also lower the cost of electricity per kilowatt-hour by 30 per cent, with one 10-megawatt high-altitude wind power system enough to power more than 10,000 households per year, the state broadcaster said.
High-altitude wind has emerged as a promising renewable energy source, offering enormous generation potential because of the much higher wind speeds and energy density compared with surface wind resources.
The wind energy contained within high altitudes alone could theoretically meet existing global energy demand by a factor of more than 100, according to studies from 2009 and 2012 led by researchers with the Washington-based Carnegie Institution for Science.
Airborne wind energy systems rely on aircraft to harvest wind at high altitudes hundreds or thousands of metres above the Earth’s surface.
These systems can be split into two categories: one in which the electricity generator itself is airborne and another in which the flying parts of the system are used to drive an electricity-generating system on the ground, according to the United Nations.
The cost of the airborne energy harvesting units could be kept low because they are often made of lightweight materials and require minimal use of steel and concrete.
Similar kite systems are being developed elsewhere in the world, including by the Dutch start-up Kitepower, whose kites are flown in continuous cycles of figure-eight patterns to produce energy at the ground station to which they are tethered.
China is also working on developing airborne turbines, with the world’s largest airborne wind turbine, about the size of a basketball court and the height of a 13-storey building, lifting off in September.
The airship-like S1500 became the first turbine of its kind to generate one megawatt of power during a test flight in China’s western Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region.
The turbine floats on helium-filled shells and delivers electricity back down to the ground using heavy-duty cables.

data.title: Meet the Malaysian Muslim trans woman whose 50-year-old secret reshapes history
data.description:
For 50 years, Sari Kartina Abdul Karim kept a secret that would reshape Malaysian history. In 1974, at the age of 26, she became one of the region’s earliest Muslim trans women to undergo gender-affirming surgery and, in a rare moment of religious recognition, married with the approval of a Johor mufti.
For five decades, she kept her story to herself. But now she says that silence has finally outlived its purpose.
“It feels like someone who was blind suddenly being told they have their eyesight again,” she told This Week in Asia in an exclusive interview.
“For 50 years I carried everything inside me. I stayed quiet for my mother and father because I felt responsible for protecting them. Now that generation has passed, I finally feel free to speak.”
Kartina, now 76, lives in the Netherlands, surrounded by the routines of retirement and a small Malaysian community she volunteers with. She spends her mornings with coffee and her two cats, and her afternoons working on a memoir she calls My Fifty Years Journey to Womanhood.

She left Malaysia in the early 1980s, but the defining chapters of her life remain in the southern city of Johor Bahru and Singapore, where she grew up and began the transition that would shape her future.
Her childhood, she recalled, was warm and protected, shaped by a large English-speaking household with colonial-era influences and a constant flow of relatives visiting the family home.
“People imagine someone like me must have had a difficult childhood, but it was fantastic. My mother understood everything about me. I never blamed my parents for anything,” she said.
“The only thing that changed me was when I was 12 and a family member abused me. After that I became introverted. I felt ashamed. But my family still gave me love.”
Whispers are far more painful than the loud insults you hear today
Sari Kartina, who transitioned in 1974
Those early years unfolded at a time when Malaysia barely discussed gender identity. She remembers the 1960s and 1970s as what she calls “an age of whispers”, a period when anyone considered different was talked about softly and often cruelly.
“Whispers are far more painful than the loud insults you hear today,” she said. “When people whisper, you cannot defend yourself. You just pretend nothing is happening and you protect the family’s dignity. That was how we lived. You hide everything under the rug and you behave as if everything is normal.”
In her twenties, she worked in public relations and lived fully as a woman. Around then she met the man she would later describe as “the one who gave me the path to womanhood”.
He accompanied her through six months of psychiatric assessments in Singapore, followed by physical evaluations conducted by Professor S. Shan Ratnam, a pioneer in gender-affirming surgery in Southeast Asia.

“It was very thorough,” she said. “They asked every question they could think of. How do you see yourself? Where do you feel comfortable? What do you do when you need a toilet? All of it was to understand who I really am. I was prepared and inside I was already a woman.”
Because she was Muslim, Ratnam would not operate without religious approval. So Kartina went to the Johor mufti’s office, where she had to explain her identity in detail.
“I had to tell him exactly who I was,” she said. “He looked at me not only physically but mentally. He said he saw a woman. Without that, nothing else would have been possible.”
‘Congratulations, Miss’
Ratnam performed the surgery in 1974. She remembers drifting in and out of consciousness in hospital until one moment came into focus.
“When I woke up, [Ratnam] was standing there,” she said. “He said, ‘Congratulations, Miss.’ I carry that memory with me, always.”
About eight months later, she and her fiancé went to the Johor kadi’s office to formalise their marriage. In Malaysia, Muslim marriages are conducted before a kadi, a state-appointed Islamic registrar who has the authority to decide whether a couple is religiously eligible to wed.
She arrived with her father, brothers and other male relatives as her witnesses. The ceremony was moving ahead without difficulty until her father decided to disclose her past to the kadi.
“Everything stopped,” she said. “We waited while he made calls to the senior clerics. It took hours. My fiancé and I did not speak. We waited for him to decide our future.”

The kadi eventually ruled that she was to be treated as a woman and could be married. Her family held a small reception afterwards.
Reporters were already waiting at the religious office and later turned up at her family home after word spread about what had happened, she said.
She tried to avoid the attention, but her father told her that speaking up might help others like her. She agreed to a single interview with Singapore’s Berita Harian and refused all other requests.
“Malaysia was not ready,” she recalled. “There was no internet, no way to explain myself. I was insulted and people said nasty things about me and my family. People heard the story the way they wanted. It was never my message.”
Her marriage lasted several years before she left for Europe to pursue further studies. The relationship eventually ended. Years later, she remarried in the Netherlands.
“The first gave me the path to womanhood,” she said, her eyes welling. “The second gave me dignity and his name.” Her second husband died three years ago.
Today, Malaysia does not provide a formal mechanism for legal gender change. Muslim trans women can still face prosecution under state Shariah laws for “posing as a woman”, offences that carry fines or jail terms.
Mismatched identity cards continue to cause problems at airports, hospitals and government offices, and the country has no administrative process for changing sex markers.

In Johor, the same state that once approved her marriage, religious authorities have tightened enforcement.
Last year, the Johor Islamic Religious Department received dedicated funding for what it described as counselling and “rehabilitation” programmes for transgender women, alongside expanded outreach and enforcement.
The initiative, which includes a centre that became fully operational in July last year, was framed as a form of help but drew criticism from rights groups who argued it intensified policing of an already marginalised community.
“Malaysia today is still struggling,” Kartina said. “People think being transgender is some kind of trend on television. It is not. It is identity. It is a person claiming back who they really are.
“But I also believe respect goes both ways. The transgender community must respect society and society must respect us. You cannot demand everything if you do not show dignity in how you live … we just want to live as women, as who we are.”
Her faith, she says, has always been her guide. She said: “People say nasty things about me. I always say, Allah knows you. Those people do not. At the end of the day it is between you and Allah. That is how I survived everything.”
When asked whether she saw herself as a pioneer in Malaysia’s transgender history, she paused before answering.
“It is not about pride. It is simply history,” she said. “You cannot erase it. I believe I was the first Muslim woman in Southeast Asia to do this. If someone looks at me and feels courage to continue with their life, then I am honoured.”
